name: Walnuts

book_claims.text: Walnut consumption is associated with lower all-cause mortality in long-term prospective studies.
book_claims.mechanism: Walnuts are rich in alpha-linolenic acid (ALA), polyphenols, and L-arginine which reduce systemic inflammation, improve endothelial function, and modulate the gut microbiome.
book_claims.reference: Bao et al. NEJM 2013 — Harvard prospective study

book_claims.text: Walnuts are recommended in the Longevity Diet as a primary plant source of healthy fats and protein for adults over 65.
book_claims.mechanism: ALA omega-3 fats and arginine-rich protein support cardiovascular health while keeping IGF-1 lower than animal-protein equivalents.
book_claims.recommendation: 1 ounce (about 7 walnuts) daily, paired with olive oil and legumes

supplementary_research.finding: PMID 24269108 — daily walnut consumption (28-43g) reduced all-cause mortality by 14% and cardiovascular mortality by 25% in 30-year Harvard cohort.
Walnuts have the highest ALA content (~2.5g per 28g) among commonly consumed nuts; thermal degradation begins around 160°C.

seasonality: harvested fall; storage stable raw if refrigerated

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: ALA conversion to EPA/DHA is ~5%; pairs with vitamin E to prevent oxidation
gut bacteria convert to urolithins; effect varies by microbiome

synergies.note: ALA + MUFA pairing produces stronger lipid-profile improvement than either alone.
Temperatures above 160°C degrade ALA. Use raw or lightly toasted only.
